methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series G → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.25x) to $252.6B total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

data.narrative:
ARC Clean Technology is a Series G nuclear energy company that has raised $50M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comp universe of 11 peers—3 public (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private entities including TerraPower, X-Energy, Kairos Power, and Nano Nuclear Energy. The narrow peer set within nuclear energy limits the applicability of traditional multiples-based approaches.

Stage-progression methodology proves the only viable triangulation method. Using the $50M+ total raised as a proxy for comparable early-stage capitalization, a Series G to next-stage step-up multiplier of 1.25x yields an implied valuation of $315.8B. Peer-median analysis was not applicable due to insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables within the nuclear energy sector—only two adjacent-stage peers exist, falling short of the minimum three-datapoint threshold needed for a credible median calculation. A full-sector median would introduce unacceptable noise given the heterogeneity of nuclear technology approaches and commercialization timelines.

The triangulated valuation range is $268.4B (low) to $363.1B (high), with a median point of $315.8B. This single-method result carries material uncertainty. The primary risk is the sparsity of later-stage nuclear energy comps, which limits confidence in stage-progression step-up assumptions. Refinement would require either additional late-stage nuclear raises or broader sector data on capital intensity and development timelines.
